Title: Nicotine-induced protein expression profiling reveals mutually altered proteins across four human cell lines
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Embryonic kidney (HEK293); Cervical adenocarcinoma (HeLa); Pancreatic stellate cells (RLT-PSC); Bone marrow-derived neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y)
Experimental Design: Drug-treatment (Nicotine), TMT 10-plex, SPS-MS3, Multi-cell line comparison Mock-treatment (sterile water); Orbitrap Fusion
Short Description: The study quantified 8590 proteins across four human cell lines (HEK, HeLa, PaSC, SH-SY5Y), each mock- or nicotine-treated, using TMT 10-plex and SPS-MS3 to profile nicotine-induced proteomic alterations.
Normalization Strategy: Summed reporter ion intensity normalization per channel
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10plex (K, Protein N-term). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: Nicotine increased 31 proteins (e.g., APP, APLP2) across all lines, highlighting stress response in autophagy

Title: Isoelectric point-based fractionation by HiRIEF coupled to LC-MS allows for in-depth quantitative analysis of the phosphoproteome
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: HeLa human cervix adenocarcinoma cell line; Epithelial cell line; harvested in exponential growth phase
Experimental Design: TMT 10-plex non-phospho HiRIEF (pH 3–10), asynchronous untreated HeLa cell line
Short Description: Non-phospho HiRIEF LC-MS/MS TMT 10-plex dataset on asynchronous untreated HeLa cervix adenocarcinoma cells; from full 10-plex design only 4 untreated channels retained for atlas
TMT Channels Used: 126 (Control 1); 127N (Control 2); 127C (Control 3); 128N (Control 4)
Normalization Strategy: Reporter ion median normalization per channel
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10plex (K, Protein N-term). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: Identified 22,712 phosphorylation sites via HiRIEF, including 1,264 novel sites functional in mitosis

Experimental Design: Case-control, perturbation (overexpression/knockdown), multi-omics (proteomics/phosphoproteomics), SILAC, TMT batch

Title: Proteomics Profiling of CLL Versus Healthy B-cells Identifies Putative Therapeutic Targets and a Subtype-independent Signature of Spliceosome Dysregulation
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Isolated B-cells (B-cell material) from PBMCs of CLL patients and healthy donors
Disease Subtype: IGHV-mutated, IGHV-unmutated, Trisomy 12, NOTCH1-mutant, SF3B1-mutant, CD38+
Experimental Design: Case-control, TMT batch, quantitative proteomics
Short Description: Discovery-stage study using TMT 10-plex and LC-MS to characterize isolated B-cells from CLL patients and healthy donors to identify disease-specific protein signatures
Proteins Quantified: 8694 identified (q < 0.01); 5956 relatively quantified across all samples
Samples Used N: 16 (CLL sample 4621 excluded as a statistical outlier)
Normalization Strategy: SPIQuE (weighted PSM contributions); Regulation score (Rs); Batch effect reduction via bridging controls; platelet protein filtering

Title: Organoids model transcriptional hallmarks of oncogenic KRAS activation in lung epithelial progenitor cells
Case Cancer Treated: 4 hiPSC-derived lung epithelial organoids (iAT2), 15 days Doxycycline (1 µg/ml) induction; 4 biological replicates
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: iAT2 organoids (NKX2-1^GFP+ / SFTPC^tdTomato+), derived from human iPSCs
Experimental Design: Case-control proteomic and phosphoproteomic profiling using TMT-10plex labeling. Eight samples were tryptically digested, labeled with TMT 10-plex tags and pooled. Peptide mixture was fractionated via basic reversed-phase HPLC into 12 fractions, and each fraction was split to both directly analyze proteome and be individually phosphoenriched.. Drug-treatment (doxycycline induction vs DMSO vehicle); case–control; TMT-based quantitative proteomics and phosphoproteomics
Short Description: Deep proteome and phosphoproteome profiling of human iAT2 organoids carrying inducible KRASG12D, comparing untreated cancer-model controls (DMSO) to doxycycline-induced KRASG12D activation using TMT-based LC-MS/MS
TMT Channels Used: Reporter intensity corrected 1 Control1 DMSO (control) Reporter intensity corrected 2 Control2 DMSO (control) Reporter intensity corrected 3 Control3 DMSO (control) Reporter intensity corrected 4 Control4 DMSO (control)
TMT Channels Comparison: Reporter intensity corrected 5 KRASmut1 dox-induced KRASG12D Reporter intensity corrected 6 KRASmut2 dox-induced KRASG12D Reporter intensity corrected 7 KRASmut3 dox-induced KRASG12D Reporter intensity corrected 8 KRASmutG12D4 dox-induced KRASG12D
Normalization Strategy: TMT-based reporter ion normalization (MaxQuant)
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C). Variable: Oxidation (M); Acetylation (Protein N-term)
Main_Finding: Proteomic and phosphoproteomic confirmation of KRAS pathway activation in human iAT2 lung cancer model organoids

Title: Microscopy-based single-cell proteomic profiling reveals heterogeneity in DNA damage response dynamics
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Single U2OS human osteosarcoma cells after ionizing radiation (DDR phenotypes)
Experimental Design: Single-cell proteomics with microscopy-guided cell selection; TMT10plex SCoPE-MS; comparison of single cells with normal vs abnormal DNA damage response dynamics
Short Description: FUNpro microscopy-guided single-cell proteomics workflow was used to isolate and profile 80 irradiated U2OS cells classified by DDR dynamics (40 normal repair vs 40 persistent DDR). TMT10plex SCoPE-MS was applied to quantify proteins and identify regulators of DDR heterogeneity including PDS5A.

Title: Proteotranscriptomics of ocular adnexal B-cell lymphoma reveals an oncogenic role of alternative splicing and identifies a diagnostic marker

Title: Quantitative proteomics identifies conserved proteins and altered regulation of mucin-16 in low grade serous ovarian cancers
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: 12 FFPE LGSOC tumors; 24 FFPE HGSOC tumors; 12 benign FFPE fallopian tube tissues; laser microdissected tumor epithelium / whole tissue equivalents
Tissue: Ovarian serous carcinoma tissue; benign fallopian tube tissue
Tumor Type: Low-grade serous ovarian carcinoma; High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma
Experimental Design: Case-control; discovery cohort + independent validation; multiplexed TMT11 proteomics
Short Description: Quantitative TMT11 LC-MS/MS proteomics of 48 FFPE tissues comparing LGSOC, HGSOC and benign fallopian tube to identify conserved subtype-specific proteins including MUC16.
TMT Channels Comparison: LGSOC vs HGSOC; LGSOC vs FT; HGSOC vs FT
TMT Additional Channels: Pooled internal reference (126) + partially unused channels across some plexes
Normalization Strategy: Reporter ion intensity-based protein normalization (not fully specified)
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl(C); TMT11plex(K); TMT11plex(N-term) Variable: Oxidation(M); Hydroxylation
Main_Finding: LGSOC and HGSOC have distinct proteomic signatures; 275 conserved differential proteins validated; LGSOC showed elevated MUC16/CD73 and adhesion-related programs, HGSOC cell-cycle programs.
Data_Caveats: Small discovery cohort; FFPE material; uneven TMT channel occupancy; normalization details incompletely described.

Title: Proteogenomic characterization of highly enriched viable leukemic blasts in acute myeloid leukemia: A SWOG report
Patients / donors: Mixed / repeated AML channel entries (not unique patients)
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: BLAST CD38- = less differentiated blast-enriched fraction; BLAST CD38+ = more differentiated / activated blast fraction
Experimental Design: Sorted blast-state proteomics
Short Description: Integrated blast-state channels across multiple plates/files
TMT Channels Used: 2074 annotated blast channels
Normalization Strategy: Reporter ion intensity normalization
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl(C); TMT10/11plex(K); TMT10/11plex(N-term) Variable: Oxidation(M)
Main_Finding: CD38-defined blast states show distinct AML proteomic programs
Data_Caveats: Channel entries are not unique patients; repeated files/fractions present

Title: Proteogenomic characterization of highly enriched viable leukemic blasts in acute myeloid leukemia: A SWOG report
Patients / donors: Mixed / repeated AML channel entries (not unique patients)
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: LSC = leukemic stem cell-enriched fraction with primitive self-renewal-like phenotype
Experimental Design: Sorted stem-like proteomics
Short Description: Integrated LSC channels across multiple plates/files
TMT Channels Used: 259 annotated LSC channels
Normalization Strategy: Reporter ion intensity normalization
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl(C); TMT10/11plex(K); TMT10/11plex(N-term) Variable: Oxidation(M)
Main_Finding: Stem-like leukemic compartment has distinct proteomic state
Data_Caveats: Channel entries are not unique patients; pooled/repeated material possible

Title: Proteogenomic characterization of highly enriched viable leukemic blasts in acute myeloid leukemia: A SWOG report
Patients / donors: Mixed / repeated AML channel entries (not unique patients)
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: PROG = progenitor-like leukemic fraction intermediate between stem-like LSC and blast states
Experimental Design: Sorted progenitor-state proteomics
Short Description: Integrated progenitor-state channels across multiple plates/files
TMT Channels Used: 181 annotated PROG channels
Normalization Strategy: Reporter ion intensity normalization
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl(C); TMT10/11plex(K); TMT10/11plex(N-term) Variable: Oxidation(M)
Main_Finding: Intermediate progenitor-like state differs from blast and stem-like states
Data_Caveats: Channel entries are not unique patients; pooled/repeated material possible

Title: Proteogenomic characterization of highly enriched viable leukemic blasts in acute myeloid leukemia: A SWOG report
Patients / donors: Mixed / repeated AML channel entries (not unique patients)
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: BULK MNC = unsorted mononuclear cell fraction containing leukemic blasts plus normal immune/background cells
Experimental Design: Unsorted bulk AML proteomics
Short Description: Integrated bulk channels representing mixed specimen cellularity
TMT Channels Used: 2003 annotated BULK channels
Normalization Strategy: Reporter ion intensity normalization
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl(C); TMT10/11plex(K); TMT10/11plex(N-term) Variable: Oxidation(M)
Main_Finding: Bulk samples capture mixed AML biology and compartment contrasts
Data_Caveats: Channel entries are not unique patients; heterogeneous composition; repeated fractions likely present

Title: Proteogenomic Characterization of Ovarian HGSC Implicates Mitotic Kinases, Replication Stress in Observed Chromosomal Instability
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Ovarian Serous Cystadenocarcinoma
Experimental Design: TMT10; proteome + phosphoproteome; LC-MS/MS; WXS; RNA-seq
Short Description: Proteogenomic characterization of 83 prospectively collected ovarian HGSC and 20 normal FT samples; identifies mitotic kinases (CDK1/2/4/7, AURKA) and replication stress as drivers of chromosomal instability
Normalization Strategy: Universal pooled reference normalization; CDAP harmonization; log2 relative abundance ratios
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl(C); TMT10plex(K); TMT10plex(N-term) Variable: Oxidation(M)
Main_Finding: Integrated TMT10 proteogenomic and phosphoproteomic profiling of HGSC identified increased CDK/AURKA signaling, replication stress-associated pathways, RB1 hyperphosphorylation, HRD-associated histone acetylation changes, and chromosomal instability signatures relative to normal FT controls
Data_Caveats: Relative TMT quantification dependent on pooled reference channel normalization; potential batch effects across TMT10 plexes; FT tissues represent precursor rather than adjacent ovarian normal controls; partial contribution of stromal/immune components in FT controls

Title: Proteogenomic Analysis of Human Colon Cancer Reveals New Therapeutic Opportunities
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary colon adenocarcinoma tissue; mostly matched normal adjacent colon tissue (NAT) controls
Experimental Design: TMT10; proteome + phosphoproteome; LC-MS/MS; WXS; RNA-seq
Short Description: Proteogenomic study of prospectively collected colon cancer with paired normal tissue; identified cancer-associated proteins, phosphosites, drug targets including CDK4/6 and glycolytic enzymes
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: First large-scale prospective CPTAC proteogenomic study of human colon cancer integrating TMT proteomics, phosphoproteomics, genomics, and transcriptomics. The study identified tumor-associated proteins and phosphosites, defined integrated molecular subtypes, linked Rb phosphorylation and glycolysis-driven immune suppression to colon cancer biology, and revealed clinically relevant therapeutic targets, neoantigens, and candidate biomarkers not detectable from genomics alone.
Data_Caveats: Cohort included only colon tumors and excluded rectal cancer; control samples were matched normal adjacent tissues (NATs) rather than independent healthy colon tissues; tumor/NAT design was not fully symmetric; relative TMT quantification depended on pooled reference channel 131; mixed LFQ and TMT platforms introduced cross-platform variability; phosphoproteome coverage was enrichment-dependent (IMAC bias); proteomics measurements remained limited by missing values and MS detection depth; therapeutic hypotheses and biomarkers were not functionally validated in vivo.

Title: Proteogenomic Characterization of Ovarian HGSC Implicates Mitotic Kinases, Replication Stress in Observed Chromosomal Instability
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary tumor tissue; normal fallopian tube (FT) tissue
Experimental Design: Prospective CPTAC2 ovarian HGSC study using TMT10 multiplex LC-MS/MS global proteome and phosphoproteome profiling with pooled internal reference channel 131, 24 bRPLC fractions, IMAC phosphopeptide enrichment, integrated WXS and RNA-seq analysis, and matched fallopian tube controls under strict ischemia-time control ≤30 min.
Short Description: Proteogenomic characterization of prospectively collected ovarian HGSC samples and matched fallopian tube precursor tissues identifying replication stress, mitotic kinase activation, RB1 hyperphosphorylation, homologous repair deficiency-associated signaling, and chromosomal instability programs
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic and phosphoproteomic analysis demonstrated increased CDK1/CDK2/CDK4/CDK7 and AURKA signaling, replication stress-associated phosphorylation, RB1 hyperphosphorylation, chromosomal instability, and altered DNA repair pathways in HGSC relative to fallopian tube precursor tissue, suggesting therapeutic relevance of CDK and Aurora kinase inhibition.
Data_Caveats: Relative TMT reporter-ion quantification may introduce ratio compression and reduced dynamic range despite deep fractionation. PDC annotation-derived sample counts (84 tumor / 19 normal) differ from publication cohort numbers (83 HGSC / 20 FT) because pooled reference channels, QC samples, duplicated annotation rows, excluded specimens, and aliquot-level accounting are included in metadata. Two normal fallopian tube samples were removed during QC filtering. Survival analyses were limited by short follow-up duration and low event count. Tumor-versus-normal comparisons may additionally reflect differences in tissue composition between HGSC tumors and fallopian tube precursor tissues.

Title: Proteogenomic Landscape of Breast Cancer Tumorigenesis and Targeted Therapy
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary breast tumor tissue; adjacent normal breast tissue
Disease Subtype: Luminal A, Luminal B, HER2-enriched, Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC)
Experimental Design: CPTAC breast cancer global proteome profiling using TMT10-based LC-MS/MS. The proteomics raw data consisted of 17 TMT plexes: plexes 1–13 and 16–17 contained tumor samples only, while plexes 14–15 contained normal adjacent tissue (NAT) samples only. Results reported in the study were based exclusively on tumor-only plexes. Samples were fractionated by bRPLC and analyzed by Orbitrap Fusion MS with pooled reference-based normalization and CDAP processing.
Short Description: Large-scale CPTAC breast cancer proteome study using TMT10 multiplex quantitative proteomics of primary breast tumors. Global proteome analysis included extensive peptide fractionation, pooled internal reference channels, Orbitrap Fusion DDA acquisition, and CDAP-based protein quantification for integrated proteogenomic characterization.
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to ref
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Integrated CPTAC proteogenomic analysis of treatment-naïve breast cancers refined molecular subtype classification beyond standard clinical markers, identified proteome-defined signaling and immune subgroups, revealed discordance between genomic and proteomic tumor classification, and identified candidate therapeutic vulnerabilities associated with immune checkpoint activity and CDK4/6 inhibitor responsiveness.
Data_Caveats: Normal adjacent tissue (NAT) samples were processed in separate NAT-only plexes (14–15), while reported study results were generated only from tumor-only plexes, limiting direct tumor-versus-NAT comparisons within the same multiplex batches. MS2-based TMT quantification on Orbitrap Fusion may be susceptible to ratio compression/co-isolation interference compared with SPS-MS3 workflows.

Title: Proteogenomic Characterization of Endometrial Carcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: The cohort included 95 primary endometrial carcinomas (83 endometrioid and 12 serous tumors) and 49 normal uterine tissue samples, including 18 normal endometrium samples, 25 mixed endometrium-myometrium samples, and 6 myometrium samples.
Disease Subtype: Endometrioid endometrial carcinoma (n=83); Serous endometrial carcinoma (n=12)
Experimental Design: Prospective CPTAC UCEC multi-omics study integrating TMT10-based proteome, phosphoproteome, acetylome, WXS/WGS, RNA-seq, miRNA-seq, and DNA methylation profiling of primary endometrial carcinomas and normal uterine tissues
Short Description: Comprehensive proteogenomic characterization of 95 treatment-naïve endometrial carcinomas (83 endometrioid, 12 serous) and 49 normal uterine tissues identifying subtype-specific TP53, CTNNB1/Wnt, DDR, acetylation, EMT/circRNA, and immune-evasion mechanisms using integrated proteomic and genomic analyses
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C), TMT (K/N-term); Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: The genomic characteristics of the prospective EC cohort were largely consistent with TCGA-defined molecular subtypes, while integrated proteomics revealed subtype-specific TP53, CTNNB1/Wnt, DDR, acetylation, EMT/circRNA, and immune-associated alterations with potential therapeutic relevance
Data_Caveats: PDC aliquot counts exceed biological tissue counts because of pooled reference and auxiliary channels; heterogeneous normal tissue categories were included (endometrium, mixed endometrium-myometrium, myometrium); observational cohort; single-site tumor sampling may underrepresent intratumoral heterogeneity

Title: Integrated Proteogenomic Characterization of Clear Cell Renal Cell Carcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary clear cell renal cell carcinoma tumor tissue, normal adjacent tissue (NAT), and interspersed NCI7 QC cell line samples
Tissue: Kidney tumor tissue; normal adjacent kidney tissue
Experimental Design: TMT10-based global proteome and phosphoproteome profiling with integrated WGS, WXS, RNA-seq, DNA methylation, and proteogenomic analysis of treatment-naive ccRCC tumors and NATs
Short Description: Large-scale CPTAC proteogenomic characterization of treatment-naive ccRCC integrating proteome, phosphoproteome, genome, transcriptome, and methylome data; identified immune-based subtypes, metabolic rewiring, OXPHOS suppression, genomic instability, and EGFR/MAPK/mTOR signaling alterations
TMT Channels Comparison: 126–130C = tumor samples; one plex has NAT as sample
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Proteogenomic integration identified ccRCC-specific metabolic rewiring, immune-based tumor subtypes, genomic instability-associated tumors, OXPHOS suppression at the protein level, and activated EGFR/MAPK/mTOR signaling pathways not fully captured by transcriptomics
Data_Caveats: Seven molecularly atypical non-ccRCC tumors and three corresponding NAT samples were excluded from downstream proteogenomic analyses

Title: Integrative analysis of lung adenocarcinoma across diverse ethnicities and exposures
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary LUAD tumor tissues and normal adjacent tissues (NAT); partially paired cohort
Experimental Design: TMT10-based proteome, phosphoproteome, acetylproteome, and ubiquitylome profiling of LUAD tumors and NAT controls with pooled CompRef channel, integrated with WXS, RNA-seq, and DNA methylation
Short Description: Large-scale CPTAC LUAD proteogenomic study integrating TMT10 LC-MS/MS proteomics with genomic and epigenomic profiling to characterize molecular heterogeneity, carcinogen exposure signatures, KRAS/EGFR-associated pathways, chromosomal instability, PTM remodeling, and immune signaling differences across diverse populations
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic analysis identified lung adenocarcinoma molecular subtypes associated with KRAS and epidermal growth factor receptor signaling, carcinogen exposure signatures, chromosomal instability, post-translational modification remodeling, immune signaling alterations, and early-stage tumors with advanced molecular features.
Data_Caveats: PDC000153 contains only the Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium lung adenocarcinoma discovery proteome cohort and not the full integrated multi-ethnic 2025 study cohort; normal adjacent tissue samples were available only for a subset of tumors, and bulk tissue tandem mass tag profiling may obscure intratumoral heterogeneity and cell-type-specific proteomic signals.

Title: Integrated Proteogenomic Characterization across Major Histological Types of Pediatric Brain Cancer
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Pediatric brain tumor tissue from multiple histological subtypes including low-grade glioma (LGG), ependymoma (EP), high-grade glioma (HGG), medulloblastoma (MB), ganglioglioma (GG), craniopharyngioma (CP), and atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (ATRT)
Tumor Type: Pediatric brain tumors including low-grade glioma, ependymoma, high-grade glioma, medulloblastoma, ganglioglioma, craniopharyngioma, and atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor
Disease Subtype: Low-grade glioma (LGG); ependymoma (EP); high-grade glioma (HGG); medulloblastoma (MB); ganglioglioma (GG); craniopharyngioma (CP); atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (ATRT)
Experimental Design: TMT11-based SPS-MS3 proteome and phosphoproteome profiling of pediatric brain tumors with integrated whole-genome sequencing (WGS) and RNA-seq analysis
Short Description: Integrated pediatric brain tumor proteogenomic study combining WGS, RNA-seq, proteome and phosphoproteome profiling across seven major histological tumor subtypes to identify shared signaling pathways, immune microenvironment differences, and therapeutically relevant molecular features.
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding:
Integrated Proteogenomic Characterization across Major 
 Histological Types of Pediatric Brain Cancer
 A full list of authors and affiliations appears at the end of the article.
 SUMMARY
 We report a comprehensive proteogenomic analysis, including whole genome sequencing, RNA 
 sequencing, proteomic and
Data_Caveats: No healthy pediatric brain controls; cohort included initial, recurrent, progressive, and secondary tumors; uneven subgroup sizes across histological entities; several rare tumor types had limited sample numbers; relative TMT quantification rather than absolute protein abundance; retrospective multicenter cohort may introduce batch and preanalytical variability.

Title: Integrated Proteogenomic Characterization of HBV-Related Hepatocellular Carcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary hepatitis B virus-related hepatocellular carcinoma tumor tissues paired with adjacent histologically non-tumorous liver tissues from the same patients
Tissue: Liver tumor tissue; adjacent non-tumorous liver tissue
Experimental Design: Tandem mass tag 11-plex proteome and phosphoproteome profiling with liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry integrated with whole-exome sequencing and RNA sequencing using paired tumor and adjacent liver tissues
Short Description: First integrated proteogenomic characterization of hepatitis B virus-related hepatocellular carcinoma identifying proteomic subgroups associated with survival, metabolic reprogramming, microenvironment dysregulation, and CTNNB1-associated ALDOA phosphorylation signaling
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio to pooled ref
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: First integrated proteogenomic characterization of hepatitis B virus-related hepatocellular carcinoma identified three proteomic subgroups associated with patient survival, liver-specific metabolic reprogramming, tumor microenvironment dysregulation, and proliferation; CTNNB1 mutation-associated ALDOA phosphorylation was shown to promote glycolysis and tumor cell proliferation.First integrated proteogenomic characterization of hepatitis B virus-related hepatocellular carcinoma identified three proteomic subgroups associated with patient survival, liver-specific metabolic reprogramming, tumor microenvironment dysregulation, and proliferation; CTNNB1 mutation-associated ALDOA phosphorylation was shown to promote glycolysis and tumor cell proliferation.
Data_Caveats: Adjacent non-tumorous liver tissues rather than healthy donor tissues were used as controls; only 159 paired tumor/non-tumor samples passed integrated multi-omics quality-control filtering; phosphoproteome data were deposited separately from the global proteome dataset; cohort consisted predominantly of hepatitis B virus-associated hepatocellular carcinoma patients, potentially limiting generalizability to non-hepatitis B virus hepatocellular carcinoma populations.

Title: Proteogenomic and metabolomic characterization of human glioblastoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary glioblastoma tumor tissue; normal brain tissue (GTEx)
Disease Subtype: Untreated glioblastoma with and without isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 and 2 mutations.
Experimental Design: CPTAC glioblastoma discovery study using TMT11-based global proteome, phosphoproteome, and acetylome profiling of treatment-naïve primary GBM tissues and GTEx normal brain controls. Samples were analyzed by multidimensional LC-MS/MS with bRPLC fractionation and Orbitrap-based DDA acquisition. Integrated multi-omics analysis included whole-genome/exome sequencing, RNA-seq, single-nucleus RNA-seq, DNA methylation, lipidomics, and metabolomics to characterize signaling pathways, immune microenvironment, metabolic reprogramming, and subtype-specific molecular features in glioblastoma.
Short Description: Comprehensive CPTAC proteogenomic and metabolomic characterization of human glioblastoma integrating proteome, phosphoproteome, acetylome, genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics, lipidomics, and metabolomics from treatment-naïve GBM tumors and normal brain tissues. The study identified immune-associated and metabolic GBM subtypes, subtype-specific kinase signaling networks, lipid remodeling patterns, and proteome-level heterogeneity not fully explained by genomic alterations alone.
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic and metabolomic profiling identified molecular subtypes of glioblastoma associated with survival, immune microenvironment remodeling, metabolic reprogramming, and distinct signaling pathway activation patterns.
Data_Caveats: Normal brain controls were partially derived from Genotype-Tissue Expression Project datasets rather than fully matched adjacent tissues, introducing potential batch effects and differences in sample procurement, processing, and donor composition relative to glioblastoma specimens.

Title: Integrative analysis of lung adenocarcinoma across diverse ethnicities and exposures
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary lung adenocarcinoma tumor tissue and matched normal adjacent tissue
Disease Subtype: Non-small cell lung cancer adenocarcinoma from Taiwanese patients
Experimental Design: Tandem mass tag 10-plex global proteome profiling with liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry, whole exome sequencing, and ribonucleic acid sequencing
Short Description: Proteogenomic profiling of Taiwanese lung adenocarcinoma tumors and matched normal adjacent lung tissues using tandem mass tag 10-plex liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry integrated with genomic and transcriptomic analyses to characterize ethnicity-specific molecular subtypes, smoking-associated alterations, epidermal growth factor receptor signaling, and mutational processes associated with apolipoprotein B messenger ribonucleic acid editing catalytic polypeptide-like enzymes.
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: Taiwanese lung adenocarcinoma demonstrated distinct proteogenomic subtypes associated with smoking exposure, epidermal growth factor receptor alterations, apolipoprotein B messenger ribonucleic acid editing catalytic polypeptide-like mutagenesis, immune remodeling, and metabolic pathway dysregulation linked to tumor progression and clinical outcome.
Data_Caveats: The dataset contains relative tandem mass tag reporter ion quantification with pooled reference normalization rather than absolute protein abundance values; several quality control pooled samples and non-lung adenocarcinoma annotations remain present in metadata tables; five low-quality or corrupted raw mass spectrometry files were removed and one replacement raw data file was added in study version 2.

Title: Proteogenomic insights into the biology and treatment of HPV-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary human papillomavirus-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma tumor tissue and matched normal (adjacent or not) squamous epithelium from oral cavity and larynx
Tumor Type: Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
Disease: Head and Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma
Disease Subtype: Human papillomavirus-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
Experimental Design: Tandem mass tag 11-plex global proteome and phosphoproteome profiling of human papillomavirus-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma tumors and matched normal adjacent tissues integrated with whole-exome sequencing, whole-genome sequencing, ribonucleic acid sequencing, micro ribonucleic acid sequencing, and deoxyribonucleic acid methylation analysis
Short Description: Integrated Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium proteogenomic study of human papillomavirus-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma identifying molecular subtypes, epidermal growth factor receptor signaling mechanisms, chromosome instability patterns, immune phenotypes, and potential therapeutic vulnerabilities using multi-omics profiling
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic analysis identified chromosome instability, Basal, and Immune molecular subtypes of human papillomavirus-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma associated with distinct epidermal growth factor receptor activation mechanisms, immune phenotypes, cell cycle regulation patterns, and subtype-specific therapeutic vulnerabilities including cyclin dependent kinase 4 and 6 inhibition, epidermal growth factor receptor-targeted therapy, and immunotherapy.
Data_Caveats: Cohort restricted to human papillomavirus-negative tumors; substantial molecular and anatomical heterogeneity between tumors; limited matched normal adjacent tissues after quality control filtering; tandem mass tag-based proteomics provides relative rather than absolute protein quantification; annotation tables contain duplicated sample entries and additional quality control samples.

Title: A Proteogenomic Portrait of Lung Squamous Cell Carcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary untreated lung squamous cell carcinoma tumor tissues and both matched (n = 99) and unmatched normal adjacent lung tissues.
Disease Subtype: Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung belonging to the non-small cell lung cancer group
Experimental Design: Prospective Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium cohort with tandem mass tag 11-plex deep proteome profiling of primary untreated lung squamous cell carcinoma tumors and normal adjacent lung tissues, including 99 matched tumor–normal pairs, integrated with phosphoproteome, acetylproteome, ubiquitylome, whole-exome sequencing, whole-genome sequencing, ribonucleic acid sequencing, DNA methylation, imaging, and clinical annotation.
Short Description: Large-scale Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium proteogenomic characterization of lung squamous cell carcinoma integrating tandem mass tag 11-plex liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry proteomics with genomic, transcriptomic, DNA methylation, and post-translational modification analyses to identify molecular subtypes, immune states, signaling pathways, metabolic remodeling, therapeutic vulnerabilities, and candidate biomarkers associated with lung squamous cell carcinoma progression and treatment response.
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M)
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic analysis identified five molecular subtypes of lung squamous cell carcinoma, including an epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition-enriched subtype with PDGFRB and ROR2 signaling activation, NSD3 as a candidate driver within FGFR1-amplified tumors, survivin overexpression in TP63-low tumors, SOX2-associated chromatin vulnerabilities involving LSD1 and EZH2, heterogeneous RB1 phosphorylation linked to cyclin-dependent kinase 4 and 6 inhibitor response, and extensive immune and metabolic remodeling regulated through phosphorylation, acetylation, and ubiquitylation signaling networks.
Data_Caveats: The cohort included both matched and unmatched tumor and normal adjacent tissue samples, with fewer normal adjacent tissues than tumor samples, limiting fully paired tumor–normal comparisons across the complete dataset. Bulk tissue tandem mass tag proteomics may also obscure intratumoral heterogeneity and stromal contamination.

Title: Proteogenomic landscape of human pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma in an Asian population reveals tumor cell-enriched and immune-rich subtypes
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma tumor tissue
Disease Subtype: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma in an Asian patient cohort with tumor cell-enriched and immune-rich molecular subtypes
Experimental Design: TMT10-based global proteome profiling of primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma tumor tissues using two-dimensional liquid chromatography and Orbitrap mass spectrometry integrated with whole-exome sequencing and RNA sequencing
Short Description: Large-scale Asian pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma proteogenomic study integrating TMT10 LC-MS/MS proteomics with genomic and transcriptomic profiling to define molecular subtypes, immune composition, and signaling pathway alterations
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic analysis identified tumor cell-enriched and immune-rich pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma subtypes and revealed associations between genomic alterations, proteomic signaling pathways, and immune microenvironment composition linked to potential therapeutic vulnerabilities.
Data_Caveats: Proteome dataset contains only tumor tissues without matched healthy pancreatic controls in the PDC proteome cohort; TMT-based relative quantification may be affected by ratio compression and batch effects associated with pooled reference normalization.

Title: Proteogenomic characterization of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma tumor tissue; normal adjacent pancreatic tissue; normal pancreatic duct tissue
Disease Subtype: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma; pancreatic adenosquamous carcinoma
Experimental Design: TMT11-based proteome, phosphoproteome, and glycoproteome profiling of treatment-naive pancreatic tumors, adjacent normal pancreatic tissues, and normal pancreatic duct tissues integrated with whole-genome sequencing, whole-exome sequencing, RNA sequencing, microRNA sequencing, and DNA methylation analysis
Short Description: CPTAC multi-omics proteogenomic characterization of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma identifying molecular subtypes, glycoprotein biomarkers, kinase signaling vulnerabilities, immune microenvironment states, and candidate therapeutic targets
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio to CompRef
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT1 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Integrated proteogenomic analysis identified PDAC molecular subtypes, glycoprotein biomarkers, kinase signaling vulnerabilities, immune-hot and immune-cold tumor microenvironment states, and candidate subtype-specific therapeutic targets
Data_Caveats: Low tumor purity, extensive stromal and acinar contamination, and strong tissue heterogeneity complicated PDAC proteome analysis. Additional bridge and reference tumor samples (KoreanReference and WU-PDA controls) were included for TMT inter-plex normalization, increasing technical aliquot counts beyond the biological cohort size.

Title: Evaluation of NCI-7 Cell Line Panel as a Reference Material for Clinical Proteomics
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Pooled lysates from seven cancer cell lines (NCI-H23, RPMI-8226, T47D, A549, COLO205, NCI-H226, CCRF-CEM) used as a proteomic reference standard
Tumor Type: Lung adenocarcinoma; Breast ductal carcinoma; Colon adenocarcinoma; Acute lymphoblastic leukemia; Plasmacytoma/Myeloma; Mesothelioma
Disease Subtype: Lung adenocarcinoma; Breast ductal carcinoma; Colon adenocarcinoma; Acute lymphoblastic leukemia; Plasmacytoma/Myeloma; Mesothelioma
Experimental Design: TMT10-based pooled cancer cell line reference proteome generated from seven NCI-60-derived cell lines for benchmarking proteomic reproducibility, sample preparation, digestion efficiency, TMT labeling accuracy, and LC-MS/MS performance
Short Description: CPTAC reference proteomics study using pooled lysates from seven human cancer cell lines (NCI-7 panel) analyzed by TMT10 LC-MS/MS to establish a reproducible universal proteomic reference standard for quality control, benchmarking, and cross-study normalization in clinical proteomics workflows
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: The NCI-7 pooled cancer cell line panel demonstrated highly reproducible global proteome and phosphoproteome profiles and provided broad human proteome coverage, supporting its use as a universal CPTAC-compatible reference standard for benchmarking LC-MS/MS workflows, TMT quantification, sample preparation reproducibility, and interlaboratory quality control
Data_Caveats: Reference-standard study based on pooled cancer cell lines rather than clinical tissues; limited biological heterogeneity compared with patient-derived tumors; benchmarking-oriented design with extensive technical replication; phosphoproteome and proteome generated under controlled experimental conditions that may not fully reflect clinical sample complexity; partial dependence on historical CellMiner annotations and mixed database usage (RefSeq/UniProt)

Title: The AML Microenvironment Catalyzes a Step-wise Evolution to Gilteritinib Resistance
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: FLT3-mutated acute myeloid leukemia cell lines MOLM14 and MV411 with parental state, early gilteritinib-resistant state under fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand stimulation, and late gilteritinib-resistant state
Disease Subtype: FLT3-mutated acute myeloid leukemia with stepwise acquisition of resistance to gilteritinib mediated by bone marrow microenvironment signaling
Experimental Design: TMT11-based proteome and phosphoproteome profiling of FLT3-mutated acute myeloid leukemia cell lines MOLM14 and MV411 during stepwise development of resistance to gilteritinib. The dataset includes parental control states, early resistant states induced by fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand stimulation, and late resistant states with stable autonomous resistance.
Short Description: Proteomic and phosphoproteomic characterization of early microenvironment-dependent and late autonomous resistance to gilteritinib in FLT3-mutated acute myeloid leukemia cell line models.
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Proteomic and phosphoproteomic profiling of MOLM14 and MV411 acute myeloid leukemia cell lines identified distinct molecular programs associated with parental, early gilteritinib-resistant, and late gilteritinib-resistant states, including altered signaling pathways, metabolic adaptation, and Aurora kinase B dependency in resistant cell populations.
Data_Caveats: The dataset is entirely based on acute myeloid leukemia cell line models and does not represent a clinical patient cohort. Samples correspond to experimentally induced parental, early resistant, and late resistant states in MOLM14 and MV411 leukemia cell lines under controlled in vitro conditions. Proteomic changes may therefore reflect cell culture adaptation and resistance modeling rather than in vivo tumor heterogeneity.

Title: The AML Microenvironment Catalyzes a Step-wise Evolution to Gilteritinib Resistance
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Cancer cell line: FLT3-mutated acute myeloid leukemia MOLM14 cell line under parental control conditions and resistance-associated states induced by fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand stimulation
Disease Subtype: FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia with microenvironment-associated early resistance signaling induced by fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand
Experimental Design: TMT11-based proteome profiling of the FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia MOLM14 cell line under parental control conditions and resistance-associated states induced by fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand stimulation. The dataset includes longitudinal resistance-associated cellular states analyzed across multiple TMT11 plexes and fractionated liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry measurements.
Short Description: Proteomic characterization of parental and microenvironment-primed resistance-associated states in the FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia MOLM14 cell line.
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Proteome profiling of the FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia MOLM14 cell line identified proteomic remodeling associated with fibroblast growth factor 2-primed and FLT3 ligand-primed resistance-associated states, including altered protein expression patterns linked to early resistance adaptation.
Data_Caveats: The dataset contains only proteome measurements from experimentally induced MOLM14 leukemia cell line resistance-associated states under controlled in vitro conditions and does not represent independent clinical patient-derived tumor samples.

Title: The AML Microenvironment Catalyzes a Step-wise Evolution to Gilteritinib Resistance
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia MOLM14 cell line under parental control conditions and quizartinib resistance-associated states induced by fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand stimulation
Disease Subtype: FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia with acquired resistance to quizartinib mediated by microenvironment-associated signaling
Experimental Design: TMT11-based proteome profiling of the FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia MOLM14 cell line under parental control conditions and quizartinib resistance-associated states induced by fibroblast growth factor 2 or FLT3 ligand stimulation, including late resistant leukemia cell populations analyzed by fractionated liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry across two TMT11 plexes.
Short Description: Proteomic characterization of parental and quizartinib resistance-associated states in the FLT3-mutated Acute Myeloid Leukemia MOLM14 cell line.
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Proteome profiling identified distinct protein expression changes associated with fibroblast growth factor 2-induced and FLT3 ligand-induced quizartinib resistance-associated states, as well as stable proteomic remodeling in late quizartinib-resistant MOLM14 Acute Myeloid Leukemia cell populations.
Data_Caveats: The dataset contains only experimentally induced MOLM14 Acute Myeloid Leukemia cell line resistance-associated states analyzed under controlled in vitro conditions and does not include independent patient-derived tumor samples or healthy controls. Multiple annotation rows correspond to duplicated analytical measurements rather than independent biological samples.

Title: Microscaled proteogenomic methods for precision oncology
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Core needle biopsy tumor samples from ERBB2-positive breast cancer patients collected before trastuzumab-based therapy
Disease Subtype: ERBB2-positive breast cancer treated with trastuzumab-based neoadjuvant therapy
Experimental Design: Microscaled proteogenomic profiling of pre-treatment and early on-treatment core needle biopsies from ERBB2-positive breast cancer patients using integrated proteome, phosphoproteome, whole-exome sequencing, and RNA-seq analysis
Short Description: Pilot biopsy-scale proteogenomic study demonstrating deep molecular profiling of ERBB2-positive breast cancer core biopsies to evaluate early therapeutic response and candidate resistance mechanisms during trastuzumab-based therapy
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Microscaled proteogenomic analysis of core needle biopsies identified ERBB2 and mTOR signaling suppression in treatment-responsive tumors and revealed candidate resistance mechanisms including false or pseudo-ERBB2 positivity, mucin overexpression, androgen receptor signaling, EGFR signaling, and immune-desert phenotypes
Data_Caveats: Small pilot cohort with limited statistical power and incomplete clinical annotation; several biopsies were excluded because tumor content was below 50%; phosphoproteome coverage was lower than bulk-tissue CPTAC workflows; metadata tables contained pooled reference channels (CR4/CR5), withdrawn entries, and duplicated aliquot-level annotations requiring filtering before downstream analysis; findings require validation in larger independent cohorts

Title: Proteogenomic characterization of molecular and cellular targets for treatment-resistant subtypes in locally advanced cervical cancers
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary locally advanced cervical cancer tumor tissues including squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and adenosquamous carcinoma.
Tumor Type: Cervical squamous cell carcinoma; endocervical adenocarcinoma; adenosquamous carcinoma
Disease Subtype: Locally advanced cervical cancer (LACC); squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and adenosquamous carcinoma
Experimental Design: TMT11-based global proteome and phosphoproteome profiling integrated with whole-exome sequencing, RNA-seq, and single-cell RNA-seq for molecular characterization of treatment-resistant locally advanced cervical cancer subtypes
Short Description: Multi-omics characterization of locally advanced cervical cancer identified six molecular subtypes associated with treatment resistance, stromal activation, immune suppression, EMT remodeling, and mucin glycosylation
Normalization Strategy: N/A — no processed mass spectra in PDC
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Global proteome and phosphoproteome profiling identified treatment-resistant cervical cancer subtypes associated with stromal activation, EMT remodeling, immune suppression, and mucin O-glycosylation signatures
Data_Caveats: Proteome cohort included only tumor tissues without matched normal controls; proteomics was available for only 146–150  tumors from the full cohort; strong stromal and immune cell infiltration may influence bulk protein abundance profiles; substantial histological heterogeneity between squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, and adenosquamous carcinoma; subtype associations are largely correlative rather than mechanistically validate. The study identified elevated expression of MUC5AC-associated epithelial populations together with enrichment of mucin O-glycosylation pathways in treatment-resistant adenocarcinoma-enriched tumors. Cohort consisted predominantly of Asian patients, which may limit population generalizability. Subtype associations were largely correlative rather than mechanistically validated.

Title: Refining Classification of Cholangiocarcinoma Subtypes via Proteogenomic Integration Reveals New Therapeutic Prospects
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma tumor tissues and adjacent non-tumorous liver tissues
Disease Subtype: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma classified into stem-like, poorly immunogenic, and metabolism subtypes
Experimental Design: TMT10-based proteome and phosphoproteome profiling of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma tissues integrated with whole-exome sequencing, RNA-seq, metabolomics, and organoid validation; proteome + phosphoproteome; LC-MS/MS; WES; RNA-seq; organoid models
Short Description: Multi-omics characterization of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma identifying clinically distinct proteogenomic subtypes associated with stemness, immune suppression, metabolic remodeling, and therapeutic vulnerabilities including ALDH1A1-targeted strategies
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Proteogenomic analysis identified three clinically distinct intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma subtypes associated with stemness, immune suppression, and metabolic remodeling. ALDH1A1 overexpression characterized the stem-like subtype, and ALDH1A1 inhibition synergized with nab-paclitaxel in organoid models. Multi-omics integration refined molecular stratification beyond transcriptomics alone and revealed subtype-specific therapeutic vulnerabilities.
Data_Caveats: PDC metadata contains heterogeneous annotations including reference/disqualified samples and non-iCC labels despite the study focus on intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma. Sample numbers differed across proteome, transcriptome, phosphoproteome, and WES datasets. Adjacent non-tumorous tissues rather than independent healthy controls were used. TMT-based relative quantification may introduce ratio compression. Cohort consisted predominantly of Asian patients, which may limit population generalizability.

Title: Proteogenomic analysis of chemo-refractory high-grade serous ovarian cancer
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma tumor tissues from female patients with stage II–IV disease
Experimental Design: TMT11-based LC-MS/MS global proteome profiling of pre-treatment FFPE primary HGSOC tumor tissues
Short Description: FFPE validation proteome cohort of primary untreated HGSOC tumor samples analyzed using CPTAC harmonized TMT11 proteomics workflow
Normalization Strategy: CDAP; FFPE-adjusted norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Large-scale TMT11 proteome profiling of treatment-naive FFPE HGSOC tumors identified proteomic subtypes and molecular programs associated with platinum refractoriness, including immune remodeling, extracellular matrix organization, EMT, hypoxia, metabolism, and TGF-β signaling, and enabled development of a 64-protein predictive signature
Data_Caveats: FFPE archival tissue may reduce proteome and PTM coverage; cohort includes both primary and metastatic lesions with substantial biological heterogeneity; analyses are observational and derived from bulk tumor proteomes rather than therapy-perturbation experiments

Title: Proteogenomic analysis of chemo-refractory high-grade serous ovarian cancer
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary frozen high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma tumor tissues from female patients with stage II–IV disease
Experimental Design: TMT11-based LC-MS/MS global proteome profiling of pre-treatment frozen HGSOC tumor tissues using CPTAC harmonized workflow
Short Description: Frozen validation proteome cohort of untreated HGSOC tumors analyzed to validate proteomic molecular features and platinum-response-associated signatures identified in the discovery cohort
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT11 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Frozen-tissue TMT11 proteome profiling validated proteomic programs and molecular signatures associated with platinum refractoriness in HGSOC, including immune remodeling, extracellular matrix organization, EMT, hypoxia, and metabolic pathway alterations identified in the discovery cohort
Data_Caveats: Cohort consists of heterogeneous bulk tumor tissues from advanced-stage HGSOC patients; analyses are observational and based on pre-treatment samples without therapy perturbation; partial overlap with the discovery cohort may reduce complete external independence of validation

Title: Neoplastic cell enrichment of tumor tissues using coring and laser microdissection for proteomic and genomic analyses of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
Tissue Cell Type Detailed: Primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma tumor tissues and matched normal adjacent pancreatic tissues processed using bulk sampling, tissue coring, and laser microdissection (LMD)
Disease Subtype: PDAC with variable neoplastic cellularity and stromal composition
Experimental Design: TMT10-based quantitative proteomics and whole-exome sequencing of PDAC tumors and matched normal adjacent tissues processed using bulk sampling, tissue coring, and laser microdissection (LMD) to evaluate neoplastic cell enrichment effects on proteogenomic profiling
Short Description: Comparative proteogenomic study assessing how bulk tissue sampling, coring, and laser microdissection influence tumor cellularity, KRAS mutation enrichment, and proteomic composition in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
Normalization Strategy: Median norm; log2 ratio
Modifications: Fixed: Carbamidomethyl (C); TMT10 labeling (K, peptide N-terminus). Variable: Oxidation (M).
Main_Finding: Laser microdissection (LMD) provided the strongest enrichment of neoplastic cellularity and produced the most distinct proteomic profile compared with bulk and cored PDAC tissues. Bulk and coring samples showed highly similar proteomic patterns, while LMD improved KRAS mutation enrichment, especially in low-cellularity tumors. Distinct PDAC-associated proteins including COL17A1, VSNL1, LYPD3, and VCAN were preferentially identified in LMD-enriched samples.
Data_Caveats: Small cohort size with only 3 paired PDAC/NAT cases for proteomics and 8 tumors for KRAS/WES validation limits statistical power. Bulk tumors contained substantial stromal and non-neoplastic contamination due to highly variable tumor cellularity characteristic of PDAC. Sampling strategy strongly affected detected protein composition, complicating interpretation of tumor-specific signals. Coring failed to effectively enrich neoplastic cells in low-cellularity tumors, whereas LMD produced limited material yield and may introduce preparation-related bias. Metadata structure in PDC incompletely annotates sampling strategy and replicate relationships.
